text: we described in this video a long anterior section with the use of the new infrared technology.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure and proceeds to ligate the inferior mesenteric vein and artery.
The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision, starting posteriorly using a diatomy hook and a swab to assess the correct tissue plane.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection anteriorly and laterally to ensure a safe margin beneath the rectal tumor, using precise surgical techniques to navigate around the tumor site.
The surgeon staples the rectal tube below the tumor after completely freeing the mesorectum, leaving only the remaining rectal tube visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies multiple staple lines to secure the rectal tube below the tumor, ensuring complete closure of the tissue.
The rectal stem is visible at the bottom of the pelvis, prepared for a new anastomosis between the colon and rectum. If positioned low, a side-to-end anastomosis is preferred.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a suprapubic incision, indicating the removal of the rectal cancer tissue.
The surgeon divides the mesenteric vessels at the predetermined level and assesses the viability of the remaining bowel using indocyanine green dye.

The video displays three different visual modes: the first shows a normal view with no differences, the second presents a black and white view, and the third utilizes augmented reality to highlight indocyanine green fluorescence, showing vascularized areas in green and non-vascularized areas in black or normal light.
The surgeon observes a clear cut-off point in the bowel, confirming the viability of the tissue intended for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a low situate anastomosis between the colon and the rectum, assessing the anastomosis and microvascularization using augmented reality views. The screen displays three views: normal, black and white, and augmented reality with endocyanin in green indicating vascularization.

Following the intravenous injection of endocyanin, the signal first appears on the colonic side, highlighting the staple line in normal light. The rectal stump, appearing pale, especially post-irradiation, shows a clear microvascularization pattern with visible microvessels along its surface.
In case of a doubt, it is always possible to assess from the inside with a spatial rectoscope.
If we are satisfied with the surgery and the microvascularization pattern, we can then end the surgery.
Usually, we perform for low anterior resection after irradiation and a protective ileostomy as well as a drainage of the pelvic area.
The final view shows the anastomosis in enhanced imaging, highlighting the surgical site with improved visualization of the tissue and vascular structures.
Further study are needed to define if we still need in case a very good signal of a drain or an idiostomy.
